sections.label: TOOL CALL: python

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation

print("AFTER:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('M', 'G', 'W') or n['pixels'] in (20, 21, 12, 8, 6):
        print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, pos={n['boundary'][0]}")

print("\nBEFORE:")
for n in before['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('M', 'G', 'W') or n['pixels'] in (20, 21, 12, 8, 6):
        print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, pos={n['boundary'][0]}")
</parameter>
</function>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I understand! Clicking on the purple square body (not the magenta center) moves both the white diamond AND the purple square along the path together!
Before: white at [19, 39], purple at [38, 32]
After: white at [37, 34], purple at [47, 29]
So there are two different actions:
1. Click magenta center → moves purple square only (white diamond stays)
2. Click purple square body → moves both white diamond and purple square together
This is interesting. Let me continue clicking the purple square body to move both objects toward the dark gray diamond.

- Dark gray still at [57,27]
- This is different from clicking magenta center (which only moves purple square).
